data.title: Apple marketing chief for Watch, AirPods, Home to retire
data.description:
Apple’s (AAPL) marketing chief overseeing Apple Watch, AirPods and its home products is set to retire, marking a key leadership change for the company’s devices business.

Stan Ng, who has worked at Apple for 31 years, said Thursday that he is leaving the company. In his role, he helped devise the original Apple Watch and has overseen the development of subsequent versions of the device along with other notable accessories.

data.title: Century Aluminum starts expanded production at South Carolina smelter to lift U.S. output by 10%
data.description:
Century Aluminum (CENX) said late Thursday it began production of the first hot metal from the expansion project at its South Carolina plant in Mt. Holly, which will boost total U.S. primary aluminum output by ~10%.

Century (CENX) said the project, which is expected to reach full production by the end of June, increases the company's leading position as the largest U.S. producer of primary aluminum.

U.S. aluminum prices have surged following President Trump's Section 232 tariff on imports of the metal, pushing up costs for domestic consumers but driving record investment in American aluminum production.

Century (CENX) is also partnering with Emirates Global Aluminum to build the first primary aluminum smelter in the U.S. since 1980; the new Oklahoma-based smelter will create another 750K metric tons of primary aluminum production, doubling current U.S. capacity.

data.title: Friday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
11:30 AM Mary Daly Speaks

San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly participates in a moderated conversation on the U.S. economy and monetary policy before the University of California at Berkeley Fisher Center for Real Estate & Urban Economics Spring 2026 Policy Advisory Board meeting.

12:15 PM Thomas Barkin Speaks

Richmond Federal Reserve Bank President Thomas Barkin speaks before the 2026 CDI Citadel Directors Institute hosted by the Tommy and Victoria Baker School of Business at The Citadel.

1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count

The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs. 

2:00 PM Christopher Waller Speaks

Federal Reserve Board Governor Christopher Waller speaks on the economic outlook before the Auburn University Department of Economics David Kaserman Memorial Lecture event.

data.title: Trump admin urges Exxon, Chevron, other oil cos to boost drilling amid Iran war
data.description:
Officials in the Trump administration have reportedly been pressing U.S. oil producers to increase output, underscoring a message that has grown more urgent amid the ongoing conflict with Iran.

Interior Secretary Doug Burgum and Energy Secretary Chris Wright made their plea during a videoconference on Thursday with roughly a dozen oil executives, including representatives of Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX), and Continental Resources Inc.

Paralysis in the Strait, combined with damage to Gulf energy facilities, has yanked an estimated 16 million barrels per day of crude from the world market, sending oil and gasoline prices soaring. That, in turn, has created an acute political risk for Trump’s Republicans ahead of the November midterm elections.

Other companies represented in the meeting Thursday included Hilcorp Energy, Diamondback Energy (FANG), Devon Energy (DVN), and Occidental Petroleum (OXY).

While futures contracts for global crude were at $98.30 Thursday, traders are bidding far higher than that for physical deliveries.

“They’re not very high, if you look at what they were supposed to be, in order to get rid of a nuclear weapon, with the danger that entails,” Trump told reporters Thursday at the White House.

Details of the 40-minute meeting were shared by an administration official and people familiar with the matter who asked not to be named because the conversation was private. The virtual gathering was subdued, the report said. Oil executives largely refrained from relaying those concerns during the call, according to the people. Some executives volunteered that they were boosting production.

“It was a great call,” Burgum said at a Semafor event later Thursday, Bloomberg reported. “These guys are all leaning in because they’re getting a price signal that this is a time to invest.”

Wright and Burgum told executives they were thankful for increased US oil production, but stressed the opportunity to crank output up even further.

data.title: OpenAI to spend more than $20B on Cerebras chips, Information reports
data.description:
OpenAI (OPENAI) has agreed to pay chip designer Cerebras (CBRS) more than $20B to use servers powered by the firm’s chips over the next three years, The Information reported.

As part of the agreement, OpenAI will receive warrants for a minority portion of Cerebras’ shares, and that ownership could increase as it spends more. OpenAI has also agreed to provide Cerebras (CBRS) with around $1B to fund the development of data centers that would run its AI products, the report said.

Microsoft-backed (MSFT) OpenAI’s spending over the next three years could reach $30B, which could give OpenAI warrants for 10% of the firm.

Cerebras could reveal part of its previously undisclosed arrangement as soon as Friday, when it unveils paperwork to go public, the report adds. AMD-backed (AMD) Cerebras is also targeting a fundraise of about $3B in an offering next month at a valuation of around $35B. In addition to Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD, Cerebras competes with Groq.

data.title: Netflix targets 12%-14% 2026 revenue growth and $3B in ads while maintaining 31.5% margin guide
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Netflix (NFLX) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Gregory Peters (Co-CEO Gregory Peters) said Netflix is maintaining its 2026 framework, stating, "we are maintaining our guidance, our strong outlook for organic growth that we established for 2026. That's revenue growth of 12% to 14%, operating margin at 31.5%." He added the outlook "includes roughly doubling the advertising business to about USD 3 billion," while framing runway with, "we're still under 45% penetrated" of an estimated "roughly 800 million" addressable households, "captured about 7% of addressable revenue" of an estimated "USD 670 billion," and "account for only 5% of TV view share globally."

  • Theodore Sarandos (Co-CEO Theodore Sarandos) described three priorities and tied them to growth, saying, "we're focused on 3 big priorities" including expanding beyond core series and films with "further expansion into podcasts," "more regional live sports events," and "growing our games offering, including a brand-new kids gaming app." He also said Netflix is "leveraging technology" including "now even how content is created and produced," and "improving monetization" through distribution, pricing plans, and advertising.

  • Spencer Neumann (Chief Financial Officer Spencer Neumann) addressed the abandoned Warner Bros. transaction costs, saying, "there's no material impact on our operating margin outlook," and that M&A-related expenses remain "still in the ballpark" of the company’s prior projection that "was carrying $275 million of kind of cost for M&A-related activity." He also said the InterPositive acquisition had been embedded in earlier guidance, and for Warner Bros. costs, "some that we were planning to carry into '27 were pulled forward into 2026."

Outlook

  • Gregory Peters (Co-CEO Peters) reiterated Netflix’s full-year targets, saying management is maintaining "revenue growth of 12% to 14%, operating margin at 31.5%" and that this "includes roughly doubling the advertising business to about USD 3 billion." (The provided analysts estimates data was not used because it does not follow the required Q1–Q4 format.)

  • Spencer Neumann (CFO Neumann) characterized the Warner Bros. deal’s termination as not changing expense trajectory versus prior expectations, stating, "we're still in the ballpark" on M&A-related expenses and, "there's no material impact on our operating margin outlook."

  • On pricing, Gregory Peters (Co-CEO Peters) said the U.S. price changes were planned and embedded in the year view: "our initial full year guidance factors in the pricing adjustments that we expect to make throughout the year," adding, "it's very rare that we have an unexpected or call it, surprise pricing change."

Financial results

  • The Q1 2026 transcript excerpt provided does not include quarterly revenue, EPS, operating income, free cash flow, or margin results, beyond management’s references to guidance and selected operating metrics.

  • On engagement, Gregory Peters (Co-CEO Peters) said, "in Q1, view hours were up at a similar rate of growth to what we saw in the second half of 2025," and added that the company’s "primary member quality metric" "hit another all-time high."

  • Spencer Neumann (CFO Neumann) pointed to retention signals, saying, "Every region was better year-over-year" on retention and that this was "really encouraging" for perceived value.

Q&A

  • Robert Fishman, MoffettNathanson: asked about full-year margin guidance and incremental 2026 investments given Warner Bros. deal costs; Gregory Peters (Co-CEO Peters) replied, "we are maintaining our guidance" at "revenue growth of 12% to 14%, operating margin at 31.5%" including ads to "about USD 3 billion," and Spencer Neumann (CFO Neumann) added, "there's no material impact on our operating margin outlook."

  • Sean Diffley, Morgan Stanley: asked what Netflix learned from Warner Bros. and whether it changes M&A appetite/capital structure; Theodore Sarandos (Co-CEO Sarandos) said the WB deal was "a nice to have, not a need to have," that Netflix "built our M&A muscle," and that "we do come through this with no change in our capital allocation philosophy," including returning cash "through share repurchase."

  • Vikram Kesavabhotla, Baird: asked about the engagement quality metric in 2026; Gregory Peters (Co-CEO Peters) said the metric "hit another all-time high" and said Netflix will not disclose methodology: "we're not going to give it to them."

  • Rich Greenfield, LightShed Partners: asked about Nielsen’s methodology change and ad revenue implications; Gregory Peters (Co-CEO Peters) said, "The Nielsen gauge is not the currency for the video marketplace" and "We continue to expect to deliver that $3 billion in advertising revenue this year."

  • John Hodulik, UBS: asked about World Baseball Classic viewership and live opportunities; Theodore Sarandos (Co-CEO Sarandos) said it was "31.4 million viewers" and "the most watched program we've ever had in Japan," adding it drove "the largest single sign-up day ever in Japan" and that Japan "led our Q1 member growth around the world."

  • Dan Salmon, New Street Research: asked about advertiser base and programmatic mix; Gregory Peters (Co-CEO Peters) said programmatic is "on its way to becoming more than 50% of our non-live ads business" and that the advertiser base "grew over 70% year-to-year in 2025 to be more than 4,000 advertisers."

  • Eric Sheridan, Goldman Sachs: asked about gaming learnings and investment; Gregory Peters (Co-CEO Peters) said gaming is a market of "about $150 billion in consumer spend ex China, ex Russia," that acquisition impact has "really been small to date," and "we're going to continue to ramp our investment" based on "demonstrated performance."

  • Rich Greenfield, LightShed Partners: asked if Reed Hastings’ board departure timing related to Warner Bros.; Theodore Sarandos (Co-CEO Sarandos) said, "it absolutely had nothing to do with it," and confirmed, "Reed Hastings... has decided not to run for reelection" while remaining "the Chairman and the member of our Board through his current term."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral-to-skeptical, with repeated probing on Warner Bros. (margin impact, M&A appetite, learnings), advertising measurement risk (Nielsen methodology), and pricing/churn sensitivity.

  • Management tone was slightly positive and disciplined, repeatedly emphasizing maintained guidance and focus, including Theodore Sarandos (Co-CEO Sarandos) saying, "we are very confident in the core business," and Spencer Neumann (CFO Neumann) emphasizing, "there's no material impact on our operating margin outlook."

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management messaging shifted from integrating Warner Bros. as "a strategic accelerant" (Q4 2025) to explaining why Netflix "walked away" and reaffirming "no change" to capital allocation philosophy (Q1 2026).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • In Q1 2026, Netflix emphasized continuity in targets while reframing Warner Bros. as a terminated pursuit; Theodore Sarandos (Co-CEO Sarandos) said Netflix tested discipline and, "when the cost of this deal grew beyond the net value to our business and to our shareholders... [we] walk away." In Q4 2025, management had said it was "working really hard to close the acquisition of Warner Bros. Studios and HBO."

  • The ad outlook stayed consistent quarter-to-quarter, with Q1 2026 reiterating "about USD 3 billion" in ads, aligning with Q4 2025 statements that ad revenue was expected to "roughly double again in 2026 to about $3 billion."

  • Analysts’ focus shifted from regulatory confidence and integration logic (Q4 2025) to cost/discipline lessons and future M&A posture (Q1 2026).

Risks and concerns

  • Gregory Peters (Co-CEO Peters) cited competitive viewing pressure from major events, noting Q1 engagement growth occurred "despite having the Winter Olympics 17 days of robust streaming competition."

  • On measurement and advertising narratives, Gregory Peters (Co-CEO Peters) warned Nielsen’s change is "a methodology change" and "not reflecting any actual viewing behaviors," while maintaining the ad target unchanged.

  • On gaming monetization and scale, Gregory Peters (Co-CEO Peters) said gaming-driven acquisition has "really been small to date," and positioned investment pacing as conditional: "continue to ramp our investment... based on demonstrated performance."

Final takeaway

Netflix’s leadership reiterated its 2026 framework of 12% to 14% revenue growth, a 31.5% operating margin, and roughly $3 billion of advertising revenue, while stating the abandoned Warner Bros. transaction does not materially change margin outlook or capital allocation. Management leaned on engagement and retention signals, highlighted outsized impact from regional live events such as the World Baseball Classic in Japan, and described continued expansion across podcasts, advertising technology and programmatic buying, and gaming—including a separate kids games app—while emphasizing disciplined investment and a “builders” mindset even as it preserves optionality for opportunistic M&A such as InterPositive.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Home BancShares outlines Mountain Commerce conversion in November and targets end of 2026 for maximum cost saves
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Home BancShares (HOMB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We’re happy to have completed the merger with our acquisition of Mountain Commerce… we will not be able to start converting Mountain Commerce until November. As a result, the maximum anticipated savings will not be realized until probably the end of ’26.” (Chairman & CEO John Allison)
  • “We had a… $110 million Texas credit that we decided to non-perform this quarter… We do not anticipate any additional loss… and do not expect this loan to have any major impact on earnings, if any, at all.” (Chairman & CEO Allison)
  • “We continue to repurchase stock… If the volatility continues, we will be very active on the repurchase side… I want to buy that back… It’s about 5.5 million shares.” (Chairman & CEO Allison)
  • “During Q1, we grew the portfolio to approximately $2.1 billion… Private Credit balances peaked at just under $500 million at the end of 2022. And today, outstanding are $87 million… a reduction of over 80% in the past 3 years.” (President of Centennial Commercial Finance Group Christopher Poulton)
  • “The first quarter of 2026 was a good start to the year with $118.2 million in net income… The reported Net Interest Margin was 4.51%… Deposit balances increased $258 million… we repurchased 507,000 shares of stock during the quarter for a total of $13.9 million.” (Chief Operating Officer John Tipton)
  • “I’m pleased with the quarter. there’s not really any noise to it, so it’s just kind of solid Core Earnings.” (CFO Brian Davis)

Outlook

  • “We do expect slightly higher payoffs in Q2… our pipeline should allow us to replace those balances either this quarter or the next.” (President Poulton)
  • “I see this downward trend continuing in the Legacy Bank into the second quarter because Q2 and Q3 projected payoffs are very high. The MCB acquisition will, however, add over $1.49 billion in loans to the balance sheet.” (President & Chief Lending Officer Kevin Hester)
  • “Deposit balances increased $258 million… I would expect some headwinds in Q2 from tax payments.” (Chief Operating Officer Tipton)
  • No formal EPS or revenue guidance was provided in the prepared remarks or Q&A.

Financial Results

  • “$118.2 million in net income, a 2.09% Return on Assets, and 16.56% Return on Tangible Common Equity.” (Chief Operating Officer Tipton)
  • “The reported Net Interest Margin was 4.51%, down 10 basis points from Q4, as there was zero Event income in Q1.” (Chief Operating Officer Tipton)
  • “Noninterest-bearing balances grew by $126 million to almost $4 billion and now account for 22.5% of total deposits.” (Chief Operating Officer Tipton)
  • “Core expenses were about $115 million for the quarter… Mountain Commerce probably adds $7 million, $7.5 million a quarter to that number right now… the majority will come middle of fourth quarter.” (Chief Operating Officer Tipton)

Q&A

  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: asked if HOMB would loosen “the triple-accretive mantra” to get a deal done; Chairman & CEO Allison: “We hold pretty tight to our philosophy… I’m the largest individual shareholder, and I’m not interested in diluting myself.”
  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: asked about loan-yield move and the Texas nonaccrual impact; COO Tipton: “It was about $1.6 million impact for the quarter… about 4 basis points to NIM… production yields… 7.20% to 7.25% for the first quarter.”
  • David Rochester, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked about organic loan trend and payoff sources; President & CLO Hester: “It feels a little soft second quarter… we’re going to have to get the production in here and get it on the books.”
  • David Rochester, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked about merger margin impact; COO Tipton: “We’re still in the process of finalizing the purchase-accounting marks… expect a little pressure at least initially on the margin.”
  • Brett Rabatin, Hovde: asked about expense growth and Mountain Commerce “noise”; COO Tipton: “Mountain Commerce probably adds $7 million, $7.5 million a quarter… until… their conversion… majority… middle of fourth quarter.”
  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: asked about deposit-cost trajectory; COO Tipton: “I’m still optimistic that we can inch out a basis point or two… but… we’re seeing banks offer 4% for CDs.”
  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: asked to quantify scheduled payoffs; President & CLO Hester: “Second quarter looks like close to $1 billion and third quarter could approach that.”
  • Matt Olney, Stephens: asked about private credit outlook and when opportunity returns; President Poulton: “It feels early… price doesn’t fix credit… we’d want to see a little more capitulation… before we would do that.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, concentrated on loan growth visibility, margin sensitivity, deposit-cost competition, and credit-outlier containment, including “how aggressive would you be” on M&A and whether to “loosen” deal standards. (Multiple analysts)
  • Slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks, with defensive clarity on dilution and credit containment: “We do not anticipate any additional loss” and “I’m not interested in diluting myself.” (Chairman & CEO Allison)
  • Compared with Q4, the current call carried more credit- and payoff-specific scrutiny, while Q4 included broader industry commentary on “dilution, dilution, dilution” and the Mountain Commerce LOI rather than post-close integration details. (Chairman & CEO Allison)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 shifted from LOI to integration timing: Q4 said “we’re excited about our announced LOI with Mountain Commerce,” while Q1 said conversion cannot start “until November” and “maximum anticipated savings” comes “end of ’26.” (Chairman & CEO Allison)
  • Margin and loan-yield discussion pivoted from Q4 core margin framing to Q1’s “zero Event income” and an explicit nonaccrual interest reversal quantified at “$1.6 million.” (Chief Operating Officer Tipton)
  • Analysts moved from Q4 questions on growth drivers and deposit funding capacity to Q1’s heavier focus on payoff quantification (“close to $1 billion”) and merger-related NIM pressure. (Analysts; President & CLO Hester; COO Tipton)

Risks and Concerns

  • “Inflation is not dead… The question is how high and how long do they remain high.” (Chairman & CEO Allison)
  • “We’re being very careful on the loan side because of the uncertainty… asset class, and what this cycle may ultimately evolve into.” (Chairman & CEO Allison)
  • “There is some rate pressure… even some underwriting and structure pressure… that people have given into.” (President & CLO Hester)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a steady quarter with “solid Core Earnings,” high capital, and active buybacks, while placing investor attention on three near-term swing factors: the timing and magnitude of Mountain Commerce cost saves (conversion “until November,” with “maximum anticipated savings” by “end of ’26”), elevated scheduled payoffs (“close to $1 billion” in both Q2 and Q3), and the resolution path for the $110 million Texas credit now on nonaccrual, which leadership said is expected to have “no major impact on earnings, if any, at all.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alcoa anticipates $135M 2026 interest expense while environmental and ARO payments rise to about $360M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alcoa Corporation (AA) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We had a strong start to 2026, driven by execution, and we are well positioned to deliver a strong second quarter and full year 2026 performance," said William Oplinger (President, CEO & Director), while also pointing to continuity of supply despite "significant disruption in the Middle East" and saying, "Our flexible casthouse network continues to unlock value-add opportunities."

  • William Oplinger (President, CEO & Director) highlighted strategic items and balance-sheet actions: "We continue to anticipate ministerial approvals by year-end 2026," "We are in advanced discussions on the monetization of our former Massena East smelter site for a data center project," and "On April 7, we successfully and safely completed the restart of the San Ciprian smelter." He also said the company "issued notice to redeem the remaining $219 million outstanding of our 2028 notes," citing "our strong cash balance of $1.4 billion."

  • "Revenue decreased 7% sequentially to $3.2 billion," said Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO), and added that first-quarter results included "net income attributable to Alcoa" of "$425 million" and "earnings per common share" of "$1.60 per share." She also reported that "Adjusted EBITDA was $595 million" and said inventory repositioning "deferred EBITDA recognition on 30,000 metric tons to the second quarter."

Outlook

  • "We have 2 updates to our 2026 full year outlook," Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO) said: "Interest expense will decrease slightly to $135 million" and "our estimate for environmental and ARO payments has increased to approximately $360 million, up from $325 million."

  • For Q2, Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO) said the Alumina segment is expected to be "unfavorable by approximately $15 million" and the Aluminum segment "favorable by $55 million," while adding, "We expect tariff costs to increase by approximately $35 million" and "we expect the second quarter of 2026 operational tax expense to approximate $110 million to $120 million."

  • Compared with Q4 2025 guidance language, the company moved from Q1 headwinds tied to "the non-recurrence of Spain and Norway CO2 compensation" (Q4 call) to Q2 benefit drivers including "higher shipments and product premiums" and "the completion of the San Ciprian smelter restart" (Q1 call).

Financial Results

  • Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO) reported first-quarter profitability and key bridges: "First quarter net income attributable to Alcoa was $425 million versus the prior quarter of $213 million," "net special items of $52 million," and "a mark-to-market gain of $88 million on the Ma'aden shares."

  • On cash and leverage, Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO) said, "We finished the quarter with a cash balance of $1.4 billion and adjusted net debt of $1.8 billion," and added, "Free cash flow was negative $298 million for the quarter, primarily reflecting seasonal working capital build."

  • On segment-level cadence, Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO) said a North America inventory repositioning "deferring revenue recognition until the second quarter" was intended to "provid[e] casthouse flexibility for additional value-add product production and shipments, which yield higher margins."

Q&A

  • Carlos de Alba, Morgan Stanley: "what is the impact of the Middle East smelters reducing operating rate for Alcoa's alumina shipments"; William Oplinger (President, CEO & Director): "We are working with our customers to redirect those shipments" and "no direct impacts from profitability" beyond "API pricing."

  • William Peterson, JPMorgan: "unpack" the "$15 million" Alumina Q2 headwind and raw-material exposure; Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO): "Lower pricing volumes from bauxite offtake agreements represent $10 million" and energy is "primarily diesel," adding, "we do not have concerns at this point on supply."

  • Katja Jancic, BMO: whether San Ciprian is profitable; Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO): "The smelter is doing very well" but "we're continuing to have significant losses at the refinery" and "the refinery remains very challenged."

  • Nick Giles, B. Riley: whether conflict changes capital allocation and update on idle sites; William Oplinger (President, CEO & Director): "the conflict hasn't changed our capital allocation framework" and on sequencing, "You should not assume that the highest value will be monetized first."

  • Nick Giles, B. Riley: Warrick restart requirements; William Oplinger (President, CEO & Director): "it will require about $100 million of capital" and "we think it will be 1 to 2 years for that restart."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to pressing on redirection risk, input-cost inflation, tariffs, and restart economics, reflected in questions like "Any impact on profitability or margins" (Morgan Stanley) and "do the operations...are they profitable" (BMO).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, with confidence language including "we are well positioned" (President, CEO & Director Oplinger) and "we do not have concerns at this point on supply" (Executive VP & CFO Beerman), alongside caveats such as "may not recur" on currency impacts (Executive VP & CFO Beerman).

  • Versus Q4 2025, Q1 2026 placed heavier emphasis on the Middle East disruption as an active driver of premiums, logistics, and cost volatility, while Q4 2025 focused more on CBAM/tariffs context and the in-progress San Ciprian ramp.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The Q1 2026 call repeatedly tied market conditions to "the Middle East conflict" and said "recently exceeding $3,600 per metric ton" on LME (President, CEO & Director Oplinger), while Q4 2025 highlighted CBAM and said LME "recently reached $3,200 per metric ton" (President, CEO & Director Oplinger).

  • Capital allocation shifted from Q4 2025 emphasis on staying within the $1 billion to $1.5 billion net debt range (Executive VP & CFO Beerman) to a Q1 2026 action: "issued notice to redeem" "$219 million" of 2028 notes (President, CEO & Director Oplinger).

  • Analysts in Q1 2026 concentrated on shipment redirection, diesel/caustic/freight lags, and value-add premiums, while Q4 2025 analyst focus leaned more toward tariffs/CBAM mechanics, restart timelines, and monetization process detail.

Risks and Concerns

  • William Oplinger (President, CEO & Director) warned that "disruption in the Middle East" has included "the closure of the Strait of Hormuz" and said "more than 2.5 million tons of annual smelting capacity" are "off-line year-to-date," adding that dependencies "tighten[] supply" and raise "cost volatility."

  • Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO) flagged cost and policy pressure points, including diesel exposure in mining, tariff impacts where "Section 232 tariff costs" are expected to rise "approximately $35 million," and higher remediation-related cash needs behind the increase to "approximately $360 million" in environmental and ARO payments.

Final Takeaway

Alcoa management described Q1 2026 as a strong start supported by higher aluminum pricing and operational execution, while emphasizing that the Middle East conflict is reshaping logistics, input costs, and regional premiums. The company kept its full-year volume stance while detailing Q2 drivers: a modest Alumina headwind, a larger Aluminum benefit tied to inventory repositioning, higher shipments and premiums, and the completion of the San Ciprian restart. Management also highlighted balance-sheet actions (redeeming $219 million of 2028 notes), updated full-year interest expense to $135 million, and raised expected environmental and ARO payments to about $360 million.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
